_comment: Scenario-facing Michigan trauma protocol composite for pediatric head injury with spinal-motion-restriction decision-making. Source material: Michigan MDHHS 2-2 General Trauma, 2-6 Spinal Injury Assessment, and 2-12 Head Injury Moderate and Severe TBI. Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
condition: Head Injury with Spinal Motion Restriction Assessment
protocol_reference: Michigan Trauma and Environmental Protocols Sections 2-2 General Trauma, 2-6 Spinal Injury Assessment, and 2-12 Head Injury Moderate and Severe TBI
purpose: Guide assessment, stabilization, spinal-motion-restriction decision-making, prevention of secondary brain injury, and rapid trauma transport for patients with head injury and possible spine injury.

sections.title: General Trauma Priorities
points: Follow General Pre-Hospital Care-Treatment Protocol
Determine level of consciousness and identify immediate life threats
Stabilize the spinal column while opening the airway when mechanism suggests possible spine injury
Control major external bleeding per Bleeding Control protocol
Assess and treat airway, breathing, circulation, disability/neurologic status, and exposure/environmental concerns without delaying transport
Initiate transport according to Adult/Pediatric Trauma Triage or applicable MCA Transport Protocol and alert the receiving hospital as soon as appropriate

sections.title: Head Injury and Neurologic Assessment
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 2-12: Head Injury Moderate and Severe TBI
points: Use head injury guidance when physical trauma has mechanism for brain injury plus altered mental status, GCS less than 15, seizure, persistent neurologic abnormality, or decreased responsiveness
Assess and trend GCS, mental status, pupils, motor/sensory findings, vomiting, seizure activity, and signs of worsening neurologic status
Place the patient on continuous oxygen saturation monitoring and reassess blood pressure frequently
Check blood glucose and treat hypoglycemia when indicated
Avoid hypotension, hypoxia, and inappropriate ventilation because these worsen secondary brain injury
Pre-notify the receiving facility with mechanism, GCS/mental status, oxygenation, ventilation status, blood pressure trends, neurologic findings, hemorrhage control, and treatments provided

sections.title: Airway, Oxygenation, and Ventilation
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 2-12: TBI — Airway and Ventilation
points: Administer high-flow oxygen for moderate or severe head injury and maintain SpO2 at or above 90%
Avoid positive-pressure ventilation whenever possible
If hypoxia persists despite high-flow oxygen, reposition the airway and reevaluate
If airway repositioning does not restore SpO2 to at least 90%, or ventilatory effort is inadequate, use two-person BVM with oxygen and basic airway adjuncts
When ventilating, maintain normal ventilation and avoid hypo- or hyperventilation
Use continuous quantitative ETCO2 when positive-pressure ventilation is used; target ETCO2 35-45 mmHg when available

sections.title: Spinal Injury Assessment and Precautions
points: A mechanism with potential for spine injury requires a spine injury clinical assessment
Assess for altered mental status, intoxicants, distracting injury, motor and/or sensory deficit, and spine pain or tenderness
If any clinical criterion is present, or the assessment cannot be completed, the patient has a positive spine injury assessment
For a positive spine injury assessment, maintain spinal precautions during movement and transport per Spinal Precautions procedure
Use patient-centered spinal motion restriction and avoid unnecessary immobilization when assessment is negative and local protocol criteria are met

sections.title: Transport Considerations
points: Do not delay transport for interventions that can be performed en route
Use appropriate trauma destination guidance and ALS rendezvous when indicated by patient condition or local dispatch plan
Communicate mechanism, neurologic status/GCS, pupil findings, spinal assessment, vital trends, treatments, and patient response in handoff

deterioration_flags: Declining GCS, new focal neurologic deficit, unequal or sluggish pupils, repeated vomiting, seizure, or worsening headache — expedite trauma transport and ALS handoff
SpO2 below 90% despite high-flow oxygen — reposition airway; if unresolved, begin two-person BVM with oxygen and airway adjuncts
Positive-pressure ventilation being used without ETCO2 monitoring — add continuous quantitative ETCO2 when available
Systolic blood pressure below age target or trending down — manage shock and avoid hypotension
Motor/sensory deficit, altered mental status, distracting injury, intoxication, or spine pain/tenderness after a concerning mechanism — positive spine assessment; maintain precautions
scope_notes: Trauma primary assessment, bleeding control, GCS, pupil assessment, spinal injury clinical assessment, oxygen administration, airway repositioning, and basic airway adjuncts: all levels
Two-person BVM ventilation and spinal motion restriction during movement and transport: per scope and agency practice
ETCO2 monitoring, vascular access, fluid bolus, and advanced airway: per provider level, MCA selection, and available equipment
Rapid transport and early receiving-facility notification are protocol priorities and should not be delayed for nonessential scene care
